content.text: International accreditation through the Council of International Schools (CIS) and the Western Association of Schools and Colleges (WASC) represents more than a certificate on the wall—it is a comprehensive quality assurance framework that validates our commitment to continuous improvement and educational excellence. For Eduversal Partner Schools, pursuing CIS and WASC accreditation aligns directly with our mission to provide world-class Cambridge education integrated with Indonesian values and compliance requirements.
content.title: Purpose of International Accreditation

content.text: 1. Quality Assurance and Validation International accreditation provides external validation that our school meets rigorous global standards for educational excellence. It assures parents, students, university admissions officers, and other stakeholders that we deliver high-quality education comparable to the world's best international schools. 2. Continuous Improvement Culture Accreditation is not a one-time achievement but an ongoing cycle of self-reflection, evidence collection, stakeholder feedback, and systematic improvement. It transforms quality assurance from compliance-driven to values-driven practice. 3. International Recognition CIS and WASC accreditation is recognized worldwide by universities, educational authorities, and international school networks. It enhances student mobility and university admissions prospects, particularly for students applying to institutions in North America, Europe, and other regions that value these credentials. 4. Stakeholder Confidence Accreditation demonstrates to the Yayasan (Foundation), parents, prospective families, and the wider community that the school is committed to accountability, transparency, and evidence-based decision-making. 5. Professional Development and Networking The accreditation process connects our schools to a global community of educators committed to excellence. It provides access to professional development opportunities, peer schools for collaboration, and best practices from around the world. 6. Alignment with Eduversal's Commitment to Excellence Accreditation aligns seamlessly with Eduversal's Quality Management System, including:

items: EASE (Evaluation of Achievement Standardized Exam) for internal benchmarking
EduNav platform for evidence repository and dashboards
DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) for professional growth
Appraisal Program for teacher and school evaluation
content.title: Why Pursue International Accreditation?

content.text: Many schools approach accreditation as a compliance exercise—a massive documentation effort undertaken months before the accreditation visit, followed by relief when the team departs. This approach is exhausting, unsustainable, and misses the true value of accreditation. At Eduversal Partner Schools, we embed accreditation into our daily operations. Evidence collection, stakeholder feedback, and continuous improvement are not special projects—they are how we work. This approach means: Daily Practice Becomes Evidence When teachers maintain well-organized lesson plans, conduct moderation sessions, and track student progress systematically, they are simultaneously fulfilling their professional responsibilities and generating accreditation evidence. Strategic Planning Drives Improvement Our School Improvement Plan (SIP) and Strategic Plan (see Section 4) are not separate from accreditation action plans—they are the same documents, ensuring our improvement efforts are coherent and sustained. Stakeholder Engagement Is Ongoing Rather than scrambling to survey parents and students before an accreditation visit, we conduct regular stakeholder feedback as part of our quality assurance cycle (see Sections 16 and 17), making accreditation simply a validation of existing practices. Leadership Monitors Progress Continuously Through EduNav dashboards and termly mini self-reviews (see Section 18.8), school leadership has real-time visibility into accreditation readiness, allowing course corrections long before external reviewers arrive.
content.title: Accreditation as Embedded Practice vs. One-Time Event

content.text: The accreditation cycle typically spans 2-3 years from initial self-study to the accreditation team visit and final decision. However, schools should begin preparation at least 12-18 months before formal self-study begins to ensure systems, evidence, and stakeholder engagement mechanisms are firmly in place. Typical Accreditation Timeline: YEAR 1 (Pre-Self-Study Phase)

items: Months 1-6: Decision to pursue accreditation; initial application to CIS/WASC; assign Accreditation Coordinator; conduct preliminary evidence audit
Months 7-12: Establish continuous evidence collection systems in EduNav; conduct baseline stakeholder surveys; initiate termly mini self-reviews
content.title: Timeline Considerations

content.text: YEAR 2 (Self-Study Phase)

items: Months 1-3: Form Self-Study Steering Committee; assign Standard Champions; review frameworks and expectations
Months 4-9: Intensive evidence gathering; stakeholder engagement; draft self-study chapters
Months 10-12: Internal review; external critical friend review; finalize self-study document

content.text: YEAR 3 (Visit and Post-Visit Phase)

items: Months 1-2: Submit self-study to CIS/WASC; prepare logistics for visit
Month 3: Accreditation team visit; exit report; preliminary findings
Months 4-6: Receive final accreditation decision letter; develop action plan for recommendations
Months 7-12: Implement action plan; integrate into SIP; report progress to Board and community

content.text: POST-ACCREDITATION (Years 4-7)

items: Mid-cycle reports (typically Year 5); continuous mini self-reviews; preparation for next full cycle

content.text: This section provides the framework, tools, and strategies to make accreditation a natural outcome of excellent practice, not an overwhelming burden imposed from outside.
Before embarking on accreditation, it is essential to understand what CIS and WASC evaluate and how their frameworks complement each other.

CIS is one of the most respected accreditation bodies for international schools worldwide. The CIS accreditation framework comprises nine domains that comprehensively assess school operations, governance, and educational programs. CIS Nine Domains: 1. Purpose and Direction

items: Mission, vision, and values
Schoolwide Learning Outcomes (SLOs)
Strategic planning and goal-setting
Alignment between stated purpose and actual practice
content.title: CIS (Council of International Schools) Framework

content.text: 2. Governance and Leadership

items: Board governance structures and policies
Leadership effectiveness and succession planning
Ethical leadership and decision-making
Financial oversight and sustainability

content.text: 3. Curriculum

items: Curriculum design and documentation
Alignment with standards (Cambridge, Indonesian national curriculum)
Curriculum review and revision cycles
Integration of SLOs throughout the curriculum

content.text: 4. Teaching and Learning

items: Pedagogical approaches and instructional quality
Differentiation and inclusive practices
Use of technology to enhance learning
Professional collaboration and reflective practice

content.text: 5. Assessment

items: Assessment policies and practices (formative and summative)
Use of data to inform instruction
Academic integrity and fair assessment
Reporting to students, parents, and stakeholders

content.text: 6. Student Support

items: Pastoral care and counseling services
Learning support for SEN and EAL students
Social-emotional learning and wellbeing programs
University counseling and career guidance

content.text: 7. Staff Recruitment, Development, and Wellbeing

items: Safer recruitment and HR policies
Professional development opportunities
Performance appraisal and growth plans
Staff wellbeing and retention strategies

content.text: 8. Operational Systems

items: Health and safety policies and procedures
Facilities management and resources
Financial management and sustainability
IT systems and data protection

content.text: 9. Community and Partnerships

items: Parent engagement and communication
Community partnerships and service learning
Alumni relations
External stakeholder feedback mechanisms

content.text: Key CIS Principles:

items: Student-centered learning: All decisions prioritize student learning and wellbeing
Continuous improvement culture: Systematic cycles of reflection, planning, and action
Reflective practice: Educators regularly examine and refine their practice
Evidence-based decision-making: Data and artifacts inform school improvement
Stakeholder engagement: Meaningful involvement of all community members

content.text: WASC accreditation uses the "Focus on Learning" (FOL) model, which emphasizes student learning outcomes and continuous school improvement. WASC's approach is more outcome-oriented than CIS, focusing intensively on the impact of school practices on student learning. WASC Focus on Learning (FOL) Criteria: Category A: Organization for Student Learning

items: Vision, mission, and Schoolwide Learning Outcomes (SLOs)
Governance and leadership structures
School culture and support systems
Professional development and collaboration
Alignment of resources with school goals
content.title: WASC (Western Association of Schools and Colleges) Framework

content.text: Category B: Curriculum, Instruction, and Assessment

items: Standards-based curriculum aligned to SLOs
Quality of instructional practices
Assessment systems (formative, summative, standardized)
Use of assessment data to improve learning
Academic integrity policies

content.text: Category C: Support for Student Personal and Academic Growth

items: Pastoral care and counseling services
Learning support (SEN, EAL, gifted)
Student wellbeing and social-emotional programs
Extracurricular opportunities and student voice
Transition support (university counseling, career guidance)

content.text: Category D: Resource Management and Development

items: Financial resources and sustainability
Facilities, technology, and learning resources
Staff recruitment, retention, and development
Operational efficiency and effectiveness
Health, safety, and risk management

content.text: Category E: Focus on Learning (Action Plan)

items: Identified critical areas for follow-up
Action plan with clear goals, timelines, and success indicators
Integration of action plan with School Improvement Plan (SIP)
Monitoring and evaluation of progress
Stakeholder involvement in improvement efforts

content.text: WASC's Emphasis:

items: Schoolwide Learning Outcomes (SLOs): All programs and practices should demonstrably support SLOs
Data-driven culture: Schools must systematically collect, analyze, and act on data
Continuous school improvement: Accreditation is a cycle, not a destination
Critical areas for follow-up: Honest identification of areas needing improvement, with concrete action plans

content.text: While CIS and WASC share many common principles, their emphases differ in ways that make pursuing dual accreditation valuable: Focus of Evaluation:

items: CIS: More process-oriented—"How do we operate as an organization?"
WASC: More outcome-oriented—"What impact do our practices have on student learning?"
content.title: CIS vs. WASC: Key Differences and Complementary Strengths

content.text: Standards Structure:

items: CIS: Nine domains covering comprehensive school operations
WASC: Five categories with stronger emphasis on SLOs and improvement cycles

content.text: Self-Study Approach:

items: CIS: Domain-by-domain narrative with evidence citations
WASC: Integrated analysis linking all practices back to impact on student learning

content.text: Action Planning:

items: CIS: Recommendations integrated into ongoing improvement
WASC: Explicit "Critical Areas for Follow-Up" requiring formal action plans

content.text: Many Eduversal Partner Schools pursue dual accreditation because:

steps: CIS provides operational rigor and comprehensive coverage of school systems
WASC provides learning-focused reflection and clear improvement accountability
Together, they offer balanced external validation and continuous improvement frameworks
Dual accreditation enhances international recognition and university admissions prospects

content.text: These align naturally with both CIS and WASC frameworks:
Throughout the accreditation process, we demonstrate how curriculum, instruction, assessment, student support, and all school operations systematically advance these SLOs.

subsections.title: STANDARDS CROSSWALK: MAPPING HANDBOOK TO CIS/WASC

content.text: The crosswalk is a comprehensive mapping tool that demonstrates alignment between our daily practice (as documented in this handbook) and CIS/WASC accreditation standards. It serves three critical functions:

steps: Demonstrates Alignment: Shows how each handbook section addresses specific CIS domains and WASC categories
Identifies Evidence Sources: Lists specific artifacts and documentation for each standard
Ensures Comprehensive Coverage: Reveals any gaps in compliance that need attention
content.title: Purpose of the Standards Crosswalk

content.text: By creating and maintaining this crosswalk, we ensure that accreditation is not an add-on but a validation of our existing structures and practices.
The crosswalk table maps each handbook section to:

items: Primary CIS domain(s): The main CIS domain(s) addressed
Secondary CIS domains: Additional domains with connections
Primary WASC category: The main WASC category addressed
Key evidence/artifacts: Specific documents and records
Responsible party: Who maintains and updates the evidence
Storage location: Where evidence is stored (primarily EduNav)
content.title: How to Use the Crosswalk Table

content.text: FRONT MATTER AND GOVERNANCE CHARTER CIS Domains:

items: Primary: Domain 1 (Purpose and Direction), Domain 2 (Governance and Leadership)
Secondary: Domain 8 (Operational Systems—policies)
content.title: HANDBOOK SECTION 1:

content.text: WASC Categories:

items: Primary: Category A (Organization for Student Learning)
Secondary: Category D (Resource Management—governance oversight)

ACCREDITATION TOOLKIT (CIS/WASC) CIS Domains: All nine domains WASC Categories: All five categories Key Evidence/Artifacts:

items: Standards crosswalk table (this document)
Comprehensive evidence index
Self-study steering committee records
Stakeholder survey results (staff, students, parents, Board, alumni)
Self-study report (full document with appendices)

Continuous accreditation readiness checklists
content.title: HANDBOOK SECTION 18:

content.text: Responsible Party: Principal (overall), Accreditation Coordinator (day-to-day), Self-Study Steering Committee Storage Location: EduNav → Accreditation folder; Physical accreditation binders (evidence samples) Using the Crosswalk for Accreditation Preparation 1. Regular Review: During termly mini self-reviews (see Section 18.8), select one handbook section and verify that all listed evidence is current and accessible. 2. Gap Analysis: If evidence is missing or outdated, add to SIP action log with timeline and responsible party for completion. 3. Evidence Navigation: When writing the self-study or preparing for the visit, use this crosswalk to quickly locate relevant artifacts. 4. Continuous Improvement: Update the crosswalk annually as handbook sections evolve and new evidence is generated. This crosswalk demonstrates that accreditation is not separate from our daily work—it is the validation that we are doing what we say we do, with evidence to prove it.

subsections.title: EVIDENCE INDEX: WHAT TO COLLECT AND WHERE TO STORE IT

content.text: The Evidence Index is a centralized catalog of all documentation required for accreditation. It ensures that evidence is:

items: Current and relevant
Well-organized and accessible
Consistently maintained across the school
Ready for review at any time (not scrambled together before a visit)
content.title: Purpose of the Evidence Index

content.text: By maintaining a comprehensive Evidence Index, we prevent last-minute document hunts and demonstrate that quality assurance is embedded in our operations.
Evidence is organized by CIS domains and WASC categories. Below is the comprehensive evidence list with storage locations and update frequencies.
content.title: Evidence Categories and Key Artifacts

content.text: GOVERNANCE AND LEADERSHIP EVIDENCE 1.1 Vision, Mission, and Strategic Direction

items: School vision, mission, and values statements (current and approved by Board)
Six Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs) with detailed definitions
Strategic Plan (3-5 years) with measurable goals
Annual School Improvement Plan (SIP) linked to Strategic Plan
Strategic Plan approval records (Board minutes)
SIP monitoring dashboards (EduNav)
content.title: CATEGORY 1:

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Governance folder Update Frequency: Strategic Plan—every 3-5 years; SIP—annually Responsible Party: Principal, Senior Leadership Team 1.2 Board Governance

items: Yayasan and Board Terms of Reference (ToR)
Board composition and member profiles (qualifications, experience)
Board meeting agendas and minutes (last 3 years)
Board self-evaluation surveys and results
Board training and development records
Conflict of Interest declarations (annually signed by all Board members)
Board policies (finance, safeguarding oversight, Principal appraisal, risk management)
Governance calendar (annual cycle of meetings and reporting)

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Governance folder (Board-restricted access); Board Secretary files Update Frequency: Minutes after each meeting; ToR—as amended; self-evaluation—annually Responsible Party: Board Chair, Board Secretary 1.3 Leadership Structure

items: Organizational chart (Yayasan → Principal → SLT → staff)
Job descriptions for all leadership roles (Principal, Vice Principals, Heads of Department, Coordinators)
Leadership team meeting agendas and minutes (SLT, academic, operational)
Principal's annual reports to Board
Leadership development plans
Succession planning documentation

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Governance folder Update Frequency: Organizational chart—as changes occur; meeting minutes—monthly Responsible Party: Principal, HR Manager 1.4 Policy Register

items: Master policy register with version control (policy name, approval date, review date, owner)
All current school policies (see handbook sections for specific policies)
Policy review and approval logs
Policy dissemination records (staff handbook acknowledgment, parent communications)

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Governance folder → Policies subfolder Update Frequency: Continuous (as policies are reviewed/updated) Responsible Party: Principal, Policy Coordinator (if designated) 1.5 Financial Oversight

items: Annual budgets (RAB) and Board approval records
Financial reports to Board (quarterly and annual)
External audit reports (last 3 years)
Internal controls documentation
Financial sustainability plans
Tuition fee schedules and scholarship allocations

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Finance folder (restricted access); Board financial reports Update Frequency: Budget—annually; financial reports—quarterly; audit—annually Responsible Party: Finance Manager, Principal
CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT EVIDENCE 2.1 Curriculum Documentation

items: Cambridge Pathway curriculum map (Early Years, Primary, Lower Secondary, IGCSE, AS/A Level)
Schemes of work for all subjects and year levels
Unit and lesson plans (sample across subjects and year levels)
Vertical progression matrices (showing skill/concept development K-13)
Indonesian national curriculum integration plan (Pancasila, Bahasa Indonesia, Religion, Civics, Mulok)
Curriculum maps linked to SLOs (showing how each subject contributes to SLOs)
Curriculum review and revision cycle documentation
content.title: CATEGORY 2:

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Curriculum folder; Subject Leader files Update Frequency: Schemes of work—annually; lesson plans—weekly; curriculum maps—annually Responsible Party: Director of Primary/Secondary Education, Subject Leaders, Teachers 2.2 Assessment Evidence

items: Assessment policy (formative, summative, standardized)
Whole-school assessment calendar
Common Assessment Tasks (CATs) with rubrics
Assessment moderation records (pre-assessment calibration, post-assessment review)
Student work samples (across year levels, subjects, proficiency levels)
Markbooks and gradebooks (EduNav)
Cambridge exam results (Checkpoint, IGCSE, AS, A Level—3-year trends)
EASE (Evaluation of Achievement Standardized Exam) reports
Internal exam results and analysis
Subgroup gap analysis (EAL, SEN, gender)

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Assessment folder; Physical student portfolios (representative samples) Update Frequency: Assessment calendar—annually; CATs and rubrics—per unit; results analysis—after each exam series Responsible Party: Assessment Coordinator, Subject Leaders, Teachers, Exams Officer 2.3 Academic Integrity

items: Academic integrity policy
Student and staff training materials on plagiarism, citation, AI use
Academic integrity incident reports and outcomes
Malpractice investigations (Cambridge and internal)
Preventive measures documentation (task design, proctoring protocols)

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Assessment folder → Academic Integrity subfolder Update Frequency: Policy—annually; incidents—as they occur Responsible Party: Principal, Assessment Coordinator 2.4 Reporting and Communication

items: Reporting policy (frequency, format, SLO linkage, EAL proficiency indicators)
Sample student reports (anonymized) across year levels
Parent-teacher conference schedules and attendance records
Assessment data dashboards (class, subject, cohort levels)
Communications to parents about student progress (letters, emails, meetings)

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Assessment folder → Reporting subfolder Update Frequency: Reports—termly; dashboards—continuously updated Responsible Party: Teachers, Assessment Coordinator, IT/EduNav Administrator
TEACHING AND LEARNING EVIDENCE 3.1 Teaching Standards and Expectations

items: Teaching standards framework (adapted from Cambridge/Danielson)
Lesson expectations and guidelines
Lesson observation rubrics and protocols
Differentiation strategies guidance (SEN, EAL, gifted)
Blended and digital learning guidelines
AI and academic integrity guidance for classroom use
content.title: CATEGORY 3:

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Teaching Quality folder Update Frequency: Annually (or as revised) Responsible Party: Principal, Director of Primary/Secondary Education 3.2 Professional Practice Evidence

items: Lesson observation records (formal and walkthrough)
Coaching and feedback notes
Teacher self-reflections
Professional Learning Community (PLC) meeting records
Lesson study artifacts (planning, observation notes, debriefs)
Peer collaboration examples (co-planning, co-teaching, moderation)
Micro-teaching sessions (recordings or notes)

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Teaching Quality folder; Individual teacher portfolios (restricted access) Update Frequency: Observations—per appraisal cycle; PLC records—weekly/monthly Responsible Party: Principal, Heads of Department, Senior Teachers, Teachers 3.3 Professional Development

items: Professional development plan (school-wide and individual)
DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) participation records
Training session attendance logs
Professional development impact evaluations
Conference and workshop attendance records
Professional reading and learning logs
Coaching and mentoring records

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Professional Development folder; HR files Update Frequency: Continuously throughout the year Responsible Party: Professional Development Coordinator, Principal, Teachers 3.4 Lesson Plans and Student Work

items: Unit plans and daily lesson plans (samples across subjects/year levels)
Student work samples (formative and summative, range of proficiency levels)
Annotated work samples (teacher feedback visible)
Digital portfolios (if used)

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Curriculum folder; Physical classroom portfolios Update Frequency: Lesson plans—weekly; student work samples—ongoing Responsible Party: Teachers
STUDENT SUPPORT EVIDENCE 4.1 Learning Support (SEN, EAL, MTSS)

items: MTSS (Multi-Tiered System of Supports) framework documentation
SEN identification process and screening tools
Individual Education Plans (IEPs) and Individual Learning Plans (ILPs)
Intervention logs and progress monitoring data
MTSS team meeting records (case conferences)
EAL placement criteria and language proficiency assessments (CEFR)
EAL pull-out and push-in schedules
Language development portfolios
Access arrangements applications for Cambridge exams
content.title: CATEGORY 4:

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Student Support folder (restricted access); Confidential student files Update Frequency: IEPs/ILPs—termly review; progress monitoring—weekly/monthly Responsible Party: SENCO/Learning Support Coordinator, EAL Coordinator, Teachers 4.2 Pastoral Care and Wellbeing

items: Pastoral care policy and procedures
Counseling referral and session logs (confidential)
Student wellbeing surveys and results
Social-emotional learning (SEL) program materials
Student voice mechanisms (school councils, surveys, suggestion boxes)
Peer support and mentoring programs
Attendance tracking and intervention records
Behavior management logs and restorative practices records

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Student Support folder (restricted access); Counselor confidential files Update Frequency: Counseling logs—ongoing; surveys—annually or biannually Responsible Party: Counselor, Pastoral Care Coordinator, Principal 4.3 University Counseling and Career Guidance

items: University counseling program materials
Individual counseling session records (confidential)
UCAS, Common App, and other application support documentation
University placement data (last 3 years)
Career guidance activities and work experience partnerships
Scholarship and financial aid guidance records
Alumni destination tracking

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Student Support folder → College Counseling subfolder Update Frequency: Counseling logs—ongoing; placement data—annually Responsible Party: College Counselor, Director of College Counseling 4.4 Safeguarding and Child Protection

items: Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy
Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) appointment and training records
Safeguarding incident and concern logs (high-level and low-level)
Referral records to external agencies (anonymized for accreditation)
Safer recruitment checklists and interview records
Staff safeguarding training matrix (all staff, annually)
Student safeguarding training records (age-appropriate)
Anti-bullying program documentation

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Safeguarding folder (highly restricted access); DSL confidential files Update Frequency: Incident logs—ongoing; training—annually; policy—annually Responsible Party: Principal (policy owner), DSL, HR (recruitment)
OPERATIONAL SYSTEMS EVIDENCE 5.1 Health and Safety

items: Health and Safety Policy
Risk assessments (site, classrooms, laboratories, PE facilities, trips)
Emergency drill records (fire, earthquake, lockdown) and debrief reports
First aid trained staff list and certifications
Medication administration logs
Accident and incident reports
Health clinic records (aggregated data for accreditation, not individual files)
Safety inspection reports (external and internal)
content.title: CATEGORY 5:

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Health & Safety folder; School Nurse files; Facilities Manager files Update Frequency: Risk assessments—annually or as needed; drill records—per drill schedule; inspections—annually Responsible Party: Health and Safety Officer, Principal, School Nurse, Facilities Manager 5.2 Facilities and Resources

items: Facilities maintenance schedules and completion logs
Laboratory and workshop safety protocols and equipment inspection records
Library and media center resources inventory and usage data
Classroom and specialist room inventories
Technology and IT infrastructure audits
Textbook and learning resources management records
Asset register

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Operations folder → Facilities subfolder; Physical facilities logs Update Frequency: Maintenance logs—ongoing; inventories—annually Responsible Party: Facilities Manager, IT Manager, Librarian 5.3 Financial and Operational Management

items: Annual budget (RAB) and variance reports
Procurement policies and records
Internal controls documentation
Audit reports (internal and external)
Anti-corruption and ethical conduct policies
BOS (School Operational Assistance) funding documentation (Indonesian schools)
Expense approval logs (EduNav system)

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Finance folder (restricted access); Finance department secure files Update Frequency: Budget—annually; procurement records—ongoing; audits—annually Responsible Party: Finance Manager, Principal 5.4 Data Protection and Information Systems

items: Data Protection Policy
Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for core systems
Records retention schedule
Access control policies and audit logs
Data breach incident logs (if any) and response actions
Dapodik/SIMPKB data quality assurance records (Indonesian schools)
Cambridge exam data security protocols
Consent forms for data processing

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Data Protection folder; IT security logs Update Frequency: Policy—annually; DPIAs—as systems change; audit logs—continuously Responsible Party: Data Protection Officer (if designated), IT Manager, Principal 5.5 Risk Management and Business Continuity

items: School risk register with mitigation strategies
Risk appetite statement
Emergency response plan
Crisis communications protocols
Business continuity plan (remote learning, facility damage, pandemic)
Insurance policies (liability, property, student accident)
Tabletop simulation exercises and debriefs
Contractor safety protocols

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Risk Management folder; Emergency response plans (physical copies in key locations) Update Frequency: Risk register—termly; emergency plans—annually; insurance—annually Responsible Party: Principal, Risk Management Committee, Health and Safety Officer
COMMUNITY AND PARTNERSHIPS EVIDENCE 6.1 Parent and Family Engagement

items: Communications policy (bilingual)
Parent education series materials and attendance records
Parent-teacher conference (PTC) schedules and participation data
Parent satisfaction surveys and results
Parent volunteer program records
Parent handbook and orientation materials
Translation services documentation
content.title: CATEGORY 6:

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Community Engagement folder Update Frequency: Surveys—annually; PTC records—termly; communications—ongoing Responsible Party: Principal, Communications Coordinator 6.2 Community Partnerships

items: Community partnership MOUs and agreements
Service learning project documentation
External stakeholder feedback
Alumni relations and mentorship programs
Business, university, and NGO partnerships
Cultural institution partnerships

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Community Engagement folder → Partnerships subfolder Update Frequency: MOUs—as established; project records—ongoing Responsible Party: Community Partnerships Coordinator, Principal 6.3 Communications and Marketing

items: School newsletters, website content, social media posts
Marketing materials (prospectus, advertisements)
Media coverage and press releases
Complaints and compliments logs
Stakeholder communications archives

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Communications folder; Marketing department files Update Frequency: Newsletters—weekly/monthly; website—continuously; marketing materials—annually Responsible Party: Communications Coordinator, Marketing Manager
STAFF RECRUITMENT, DEVELOPMENT, AND WELLBEING EVIDENCE 7.1 Recruitment and Onboarding

items: Safer recruitment policy
Job descriptions for all roles
Recruitment advertisements and selection criteria
Interview records and reference checks
Criminal records checks and verification logs
Qualification verification records
Induction program materials and completion records
Staff handbook and onboarding materials
content.title: CATEGORY 7:

content.text: Storage: EduNav → HR folder (restricted access); Personnel files Update Frequency: Job descriptions—as roles evolve; recruitment records—per hiring cycle; induction—ongoing Responsible Party: HR Manager, Principal 7.2 Performance Appraisal and Growth

items: Staff appraisal policy and rubrics
Appraisal cycle timeline
Professional growth plans linked to SIP
Appraisal observation and conference records
Mid-year and end-of-year appraisal summaries
DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) evaluations
Coaching and mentoring logs

content.text: Storage: EduNav → HR folder → Appraisal subfolder (restricted access); Individual staff files Update Frequency: Appraisals—per annual cycle; growth plans—annually Responsible Party: Principal, Heads of Department, HR Manager 7.3 Professional Development

items: Professional development plan (schoolwide)
Individual professional development logs
Training attendance and completion records
Professional development impact evaluations
Professional learning communities (PLC) records
Conference and workshop materials

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Professional Development folder; HR files Update Frequency: Logs—ongoing; evaluations—after each PD session Responsible Party: Professional Development Coordinator, Teachers 7.4 Staff Wellbeing and Retention

items: Staff wellbeing policy
Workload monitoring data and surveys
Staff satisfaction surveys and results
Staff retention and turnover analysis
Exit interview summaries
Wellbeing initiatives and support programs

content.text: Storage: EduNav → HR folder → Wellbeing subfolder (restricted access) Update Frequency: Surveys—annually; turnover analysis—annually; exit interviews—as they occur Responsible Party: Principal, HR Manager
ACCREDITATION-SPECIFIC EVIDENCE 8.1 Self-Study and Accreditation Process

items: Self-study steering committee membership and meeting records
Standard champion assignments and working group records
Self-study drafts (by CIS domain or WASC category)
Stakeholder survey instruments and raw data
Stakeholder focus group and interview notes
Self-study final document (with appendices)
Evidence index (this document)
Standards crosswalk table (Section 18.3)
content.title: CATEGORY 8:

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Accreditation folder; Physical self-study binders Update Frequency: Throughout self-study process; final document submitted 60-90 days before visit Responsible Party: Principal, Accreditation Coordinator, Self-Study Steering Committee 8.2 Accreditation Visit

items: Accreditation visit schedule and logistics plans
Team accommodation and transportation arrangements
Evidence binders (organized by CIS domain or WASC category)
Digital evidence repository access guide
Staff and student briefing materials
Visit preparation checklist
Exit report from accreditation team
Final accreditation decision letter from CIS/WASC

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Accreditation folder; Physical visit preparation files Update Frequency: Months leading up to visit; final reports archived permanently Responsible Party: Principal, Accreditation Coordinator 8.3 Action Plans and Continuous Improvement

items: Accreditation action plan addressing team recommendations
Action plan integration into School Improvement Plan (SIP)
Progress monitoring dashboards
Mid-cycle progress reports to CIS/WASC
Termly mini self-review reports
Continuous accreditation readiness checklists (completed quarterly)

content.text: Storage: EduNav → Accreditation folder → Action Plan subfolder Update Frequency: Action plan—immediately after accreditation; progress reports—quarterly/annually; mid-cycle report—3 years post-accreditation Responsible Party: Principal, Senior Leadership Team
Primary Storage: EduNav Platform EduNav serves as the central evidence repository for Eduversal Partner Schools. Its advantages:

items: Centralized access for authorized users
Version control and audit trails
Searchable and taggable documents
Dashboard for monitoring evidence completeness
Secure, permission-based access
Backup and disaster recovery systems
content.title: Evidence Storage System: EduNav and Physical Files

content.text: Physical Evidence Storage Some evidence requires physical storage:

items: Student work portfolios (representative samples)
Confidential files (DSL records, HR personnel files)
Emergency response plans (accessible without digital systems)
Accreditation visit binders (curated evidence samples for team)

content.text: Evidence Naming Conventions Consistent naming ensures easy retrieval: Format: [Year][Domain/Category][Document Type]_[Description] Examples:

items: 2025GovernanceBoardMinutesMarch.pdf
2025CurriculumSchemeofWorkY7Science.docx
2025AssessmentIGCSEResultsAnalysis_Mathematics.xlsx
2025StudentSupportIEPAnonymous_Sample.pdf (anonymized for accreditation)

content.text: Principal:

items: Overall accountability for evidence completeness and quality
Quarterly evidence audits with Accreditation Coordinator
Final approval of evidence shared with accreditation team
content.title: Evidence Management Responsibilities

content.text: Accreditation Coordinator:

items: Day-to-day evidence repository management
Maintain Evidence Index (this document) with updates
Coordinate with Standard Champions for domain-specific evidence
Prepare evidence binders and digital access for accreditation visit

content.text: Standard Champions (SLT members assigned to CIS domains/WASC categories):

items: Ensure evidence for assigned domain is current, complete, and well-organized
Liaise with responsible parties (teachers, coordinators, managers) to obtain evidence
Review and annotate evidence for accreditation relevance

content.text: All Staff:

items: Submit required evidence promptly (lesson plans, assessments, reports, logs)
Maintain personal professional portfolios (teaching evidence)
Follow naming conventions and storage protocols

content.text: IT/EduNav Administrator:

items: Maintain EduNav system functionality and security
Ensure adequate storage capacity and backup protocols
Provide training and support for evidence uploading and retrieval
Generate evidence completeness reports for Principal

content.text: 1. Collect Evidence Continuously Do not wait for accreditation self-study to begin collecting evidence. Embed evidence generation into daily workflows. 2. Organize as You Go File documents promptly in the correct EduNav folder with proper naming. Avoid accumulating unorganized files. 3. Version Control Matters When policies or documents are revised, archive old versions clearly labeled (e.g., "2024\Policy\Assessment\v1.0\Superseded.pdf") and mark the current version prominently. 4. Anonymize Sensitive Data For student or staff records shared with accreditation teams, remove identifying information (names, photos, ID numbers) while preserving the substance of evidence. 5. Quality Over Quantity Accreditation teams value concise, well-curated evidence more than exhaustive document dumps. Select representative samples that clearly demonstrate compliance. 6. Regular Backups EduNav should have automatic backups, but critical evidence should also be backed up locally (external drive, cloud storage) as redundancy. 7. Access Permissions Restrict sensitive evidence (HR files, safeguarding records, financial data) to authorized personnel only. Accreditation teams receive read-only access to relevant evidence. 8. Evidence Audit Schedule

items: Quarterly: Review one category of evidence (rotate through all categories over the year)
Annually: Comprehensive evidence audit of all categories
Pre-Self-Study: Intensive audit to identify and address gaps
content.title: Best Practices for Evidence Management

content.text: By maintaining this systematic approach to evidence collection and organization, Eduversal Partner Schools ensure that accreditation readiness is a continuous state, not a frantic sprint.

subsections.title: MINI SELF-REVIEWS AND CONTINUOUS ACCREDITATION READINESS

content.text: [Content for this section would continue with termly rotation, review process, checklists, etc.]
[Content for this section would continue with SMART goals, action plan structure, integration with SIP, etc.]
[Content for this section would continue with comprehensive evidence checklist organized by category]

[Content for this section would continue with detailed role descriptions for all stakeholders] ## CONCLUSION ACCREDITATION AS EMBEDDED EXCELLENCE Accreditation is not a destination—it is a journey of continuous improvement. By embedding accreditation practices into our daily operations through: ✓ Systematic evidence collection (Section 18.4) ✓ Comprehensive crosswalks linking practice to standards (Section 18.3) ✓ Meaningful stakeholder engagement (Section 18.6) ✓ Rigorous self-study (Section 18.5) ✓ Thorough visit preparation (Section 18.7) ✓ Termly mini self-reviews (Section 18.8) ✓ Action-oriented improvement cycles (Section 18.9) ...we ensure that accreditation becomes a natural validation of our commitment to excellence, not an overwhelming compliance burden. Eduversal Partner Schools pursue CIS and WASC accreditation because we believe in:

items: Transparent accountability to our students, parents, and community
Evidence-based decision-making driven by data and stakeholder feedback
Continuous reflection and improvement in service of student learning and wellbeing
International recognition that opens doors for our students globally

content.text: When accreditation is embedded in how we work—when evidence generation is part of professional practice, when stakeholder feedback is routinely sought, when improvement is systematic—then accreditation becomes what it should be: a celebration of quality, not a test to pass. This toolkit empowers Eduversal Partner School Principals and leadership teams to lead accreditation with confidence, clarity, and purpose. Use it well, and accreditation will strengthen everything we do in service of our mission and our students' success. ## CROSS-REFERENCES TO OTHER HANDBOOK SECTIONS:

Section 2: Indonesian Compliance → Evidence for CIS Domain 8, WASC Category D

Section 9: Safeguarding, Child Protection, and Wellbeing → Evidence for CIS Domains 6 & 8, WASC Categories C & D
Section 10: Cambridge Examinations Management → Evidence for CIS Domain 5, WASC Category B
Section 11: Admissions, Enrollment, and Marketing Ethics → Evidence for CIS Domain 9, WASC Categories A & C
Section 12: Staff Lifecycle → Evidence for CIS Domain 7, WASC Categories A & D
Section 13: Operations, Finance, and Resources → Evidence for CIS Domain 8, WASC Category D
